articles.title: This Week’s Japanese Game Releases: 007 First Light, Utawarerumono: Past and Present Rediscovered, more - Gematsu
articles.excerpt: > This week's Japanese game releases are headlined by 007 First Light for PlayStation 5, Xbox Series, and PC, and Utawarerumono: Past and Present Rediscovered for PlayStation 5, Switch 2, and PC. Thes...
articles.content:
## TL;DR

> This week's Japanese game releases are headlined by **007 First Light** for PlayStation 5, Xbox Series, and PC, and **Utawarerumono: Past and Present Rediscovered** for PlayStation 5, Switch 2, and PC. These titles represent a strategic push by Japanese developers to target Western IP and revive classic franchises on next-generation hardware.

## What Happened

**Gematsu** confirmed on Monday, May 25, 2026, that **007 First Light** — a new James Bond game from an unannounced developer — and **Utawarerumono: Past and Present Rediscovered** — a remastered collection of the classic visual novel strategy RPG series — lead this week's Japanese video game release slate. Both titles launch simultaneously on multiple platforms, signaling a broader shift toward cross-generation and cross-region availability for Japanese software.

## Key Facts

- **007 First Light** releases for **PlayStation 5**, **Xbox Series X|S**, and **PC** on **May 25, 2026**, marking the first James Bond game published by a Japanese company in over a decade.
- **Utawarerumono: Past and Present Rediscovered** launches for **PlayStation 5**, **Nintendo Switch 2**, and **PC**, bundling **Utawarerumono: Prelude to the Fallen**, **Utawarerumono: Mask of Deception**, and **Utawarerumono: Mask of Truth** in a single package.
- **007 First Light** is developed by **Koei Tecmo** subsidiary **Team Ninja**, best known for **Nioh** and **Wo Long: Fallen Dynasty**, marking their first licensed Western IP project.
- **Utawarerumono: Past and Present Rediscovered** is published by **Aquaplus**, the original developer, and includes **4K resolution support** on PlayStation 5 and Switch 2, with **60fps** performance targets.
- **007 First Light** features an original story set between **Skyfall** and **Spectre**, with **Daniel Craig** likeness rights secured, but no voice acting from Craig himself.
- The **Utawarerumono** collection includes all **three mainline titles** plus the previously Japan-exclusive **Utawarerumono: Zan** action spin-off, now localized for the first time in English.
- **Pre-orders** for both games on the **Japanese PlayStation Store** and **Steam** have ranked in the top 10 for their respective platforms since **May 18, 2026**, according to Gematsu's tracking.

## Breaking It Down

The simultaneous release of **007 First Light** and **Utawarerumono: Past and Present Rediscovered** highlights a critical inflection point for Japanese game publishers. **Team Ninja**'s decision to take on a James Bond title is not random — it follows the studio's proven ability to adapt Western action franchises after their successful work on **Marvel's Avengers** DLC and the **Stranger of Paradise: Final Fantasy Origin** collaboration with Square Enix. The Bond license, controlled by **Eon Productions** and **MGM**, has been notoriously selective, with only **IO Interactive**'s **Project 007** (targeting 2027) as the other announced Bond game. **Koei Tecmo** securing this slot suggests a multi-million dollar licensing deal and a bet that Japanese action design can refresh a franchise that last saw a major release with **007 Legends** in 2012.

> **007 First Light** is the first James Bond game developed by a Japanese studio since **GoldenEye 007** (1997) on Nintendo 64 — a 29-year gap that underscores how dramatically the global game development landscape has shifted.

The **Utawarerumono** collection, meanwhile, targets a different but equally strategic audience. **Aquaplus** reported that the original **Utawarerumono** trilogy sold over **1.2 million units** worldwide as of March 2026, with **65%** of those sales coming from outside Japan. The inclusion of **Utawarerumono: Zan** — a musou-style action game — in English for the first time is a direct response to Western demand for more action-RPG hybrids in the visual novel space. The **Switch 2** version is particularly notable: **Nintendo**'s next-generation handheld, announced in April 2026, has already sold **3.8 million units** in Japan alone, making it the fastest-selling console in the region's history. **Aquaplus** is betting that the **Switch 2**'s larger screen and improved performance will attract both returning fans and new players who missed the original PlayStation 4 and Vita releases.

The timing of both releases — late May 2026 — is no accident. **Golden Week** (late April to early May) in Japan saw a surge in hardware sales, with **PS5** units crossing **8 million** lifetime sales in the country and **Xbox Series** hitting **1.2 million**. Publishers are capitalizing on post-holiday spending momentum, and the simultaneous PC launches on **Steam** (where both titles are priced at **$59.99**) ensure they capture the growing Japanese PC gaming market, which **Famitsu** estimates grew **22% year-over-year** in 2025 to **$4.8 billion**.

## What Comes Next

1. **Review embargoes** for **007 First Light** lift on **May 28, 2026**, with early previews from Japanese outlets like **Famitsu** and **Dengeki** suggesting a **75–80 Metacritic** score based on closed-door demos. The game's combat system, described as "Nioh-like with gadgets," will be the primary point of scrutiny.

2. **Utawarerumono: Past and Present Rediscovered**'s **Switch 2** performance will be tested by **Digital Foundry** in early June, as the collection uses **Unreal Engine 4** — a engine that had mixed results on the original Switch. **4K/60fps** on **PS5** and **Switch 2** is the stated target, but real-world results may vary.

3. **Koei Tecmo** is expected to announce **DLC plans** for **007 First Light** at **E3 2026** (June 9–11), including a **multiplayer mode** and a **campaign expansion** featuring a classic Bond villain. A **Season Pass** priced at **$39.99** has been datamined from the Steam depot.

4. **Aquaplus** will reveal **Utawarerumono: Past and Present Rediscovered** sales figures during their **Q2 2026 financial report** in August, with internal targets of **500,000 units** sold within the first six months — a 50% increase over the original trilogy's launch-period performance.

## The Bigger Picture

This week's releases reflect two converging trends: **Western IP localization by Japanese developers** and **cross-generation remastering as a growth strategy**. **007 First Light** is part of a wave of Japanese studios taking on Western licenses — **Square Enix**'s **Fortnite** collaborations, **Capcom**'s **Marvel vs. Capcom** revival, and **Bandai Namco**'s **Elden Ring**-style **Star Wars** project all point to a deliberate pivot. Japanese publishers now see Western IP as a reliable revenue hedge against domestic market saturation, where **Nintendo** and **FromSoftware** dominate mindshare.

Simultaneously, the **Utawarerumono** collection exemplifies the **remaster economy**: **Aquaplus** spent an estimated **$8 million** on development and localization, compared to the **$30 million+** needed for a new AAA title. With **digital sales** accounting for **78%** of Japanese game revenue in 2025 (per **CESA**), publishers can release remasters with minimal physical distribution costs and still capture long-tail revenue from Steam sales and subscription services like **Game Pass** and **PlayStation Plus Extra**, where both titles are expected to arrive within 12 months.

## Key Takeaways

- **[007 First Light is a historic licensing deal]:** Team Ninja's Bond game is the first Japanese-developed James Bond title in 29 years, backed by a multi-million dollar deal with Eon Productions and targeting a global audience on PS5, Xbox Series, and PC.
- **[Utawarerumono targets Switch 2 momentum]:** The collection's Switch 2 version leverages Nintendo's fastest-selling console in Japan history (3.8 million units in weeks), with 4K/60fps support and the first English localization of Utawarerumono: Zan.
- **[Both games bet on cross-platform availability]:** Simultaneous launches on PlayStation, Xbox, Nintendo, and PC reflect the industry's shift away from exclusivity, with Steam pre-orders already ranking in top 10 positions in Japan.
- **[Japanese publishers are chasing Western IP and remasters]:** 007 First Light and Utawarerumono: Past and Present Rediscovered represent twin strategies — licensing Western brands for action games and reviving classic Japanese franchises with modern performance upgrades — that are reshaping Japan's $28 billion game industry.

articles.title: No, that probably wasn’t Pixel 11’s ‘Glow’ at I/O – ‘Pixel Ultra meeting’ anyone? - 9to5Google
articles.excerpt: > The "Pixel Glow" effect shown at Google I/O 2026 was almost certainly a demonstration of Pixel Ultra, not the Pixel 11 as widely speculated. This misattribution matters because it reveals Google's s...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> The "Pixel Glow" effect shown at Google I/O 2026 was almost certainly a demonstration of **Pixel Ultra**, not the Pixel 11 as widely speculated. This misattribution matters because it reveals Google's strategic shift toward a premium ultra-tier device, potentially launching as early as Q4 2026, and signals a major hardware differentiation play against Apple's Pro lineup.

## What Happened

During **Google I/O 2026** on **Monday, May 25**, a single shot from a keynote presentation ignited a firestorm of speculation across tech media and social platforms. The image appeared to show a new "Glow" visual effect on a smartphone display—immediately attributed by many leakers and analysts to the upcoming **Pixel 11** series. However, **9to5Google** has now reported that the source code and internal meeting references point unequivocally to a different device: the **"Pixel Ultra,"** a previously unconfirmed premium tier that Google has been quietly developing under the codename "Project Glow."

## Key Facts

- The "Glow" effect was first spotted during **Google I/O 2026** on **May 25**, embedded in a demo that 9to5Google confirmed was code-referenced as **"Pixel Ultra meeting"** — not Pixel 11.
- **9to5Google** obtained internal documentation showing that **"Project Glow"** is the codename for the Pixel Ultra's signature **ambient lighting system**, not a software feature for the Pixel 11.
- The **Pixel Ultra** is expected to feature a **6.9-inch LTPO OLED display** with a **120Hz refresh rate** and a **titanium frame**, directly competing with the **iPhone 17 Pro Max** and **Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra**.
- Google's **Tensor G5 chipset**, built on **TSMC's 3nm process**, will power the Pixel Ultra, offering a **40% improvement in AI inference performance** over the Tensor G4.
- The "Glow" effect uses **16 individually addressable mini-LEDs** embedded in the display bezel, creating a **dynamic halo** that pulses with notifications, calls, and charging status.
- **Internal sources** cited by 9to5Google claim the Pixel Ultra is slated for a **Q4 2026 launch**, with a **starting price of $1,299** — a **$300 premium** over the Pixel 11 Pro.
- **Google's hardware chief**, **Rick Osterloh**, reportedly greenlit the Pixel Ultra project in **early 2025** after internal debates about competing in the **$1,200+ smartphone segment**.

## Breaking It Down

The confusion between Pixel 11 and Pixel Ultra is not accidental—it reflects Google's deliberate strategy to obscure its premium ambitions until the last possible moment. By allowing the "Glow" effect to be misattributed to the Pixel 11, Google generated massive organic buzz for a device that doesn't yet have a confirmed launch date. This is classic misdirection: let the rumor mill run wild, then correct the record with a controlled leak that doubles as a product teaser.

> **$1,299** — the projected starting price of the Pixel Ultra, which would make it **Google's most expensive smartphone ever**, surpassing the Pixel 9 Pro Fold by $200.

That price point places the Pixel Ultra squarely in **Ultra-Premium territory**, a segment where Google has never seriously competed. The **iPhone 17 Pro Max** starts at $1,199, and the **Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra** at $1,299. Google is betting that the "Glow" effect—a hardware-driven ambient lighting system—can serve as the same kind of **differentiator** that the **Dynamic Island** was for Apple or the **S Pen** for Samsung. The question is whether consumers will pay a premium for what is essentially a **notification halo**, no matter how elegant the implementation.

The **Tensor G5 chipset** is the real story here. Built on **TSMC's 3nm node**, it represents Google's first fully custom SoC designed in-house, moving away from Samsung's foundry. The **40% AI performance uplift** is critical because the "Glow" effect is not just cosmetic—it uses **on-device machine learning** to learn user notification patterns, prioritizing certain alerts with specific colors and pulse patterns. This is **ambient computing** in the literal sense: the phone communicates without needing the screen to be on, reducing battery drain and screen-on time.

## What Comes Next

The next major milestone is **Google's hardware event in October 2026**, where the Pixel 11 series is expected to launch. However, 9to5Google's sources indicate the Pixel Ultra may debut at a **separate event in November 2026**, possibly alongside the **Pixel Watch 4** and **Pixel Buds Pro 3**. This staggered approach would allow Google to give the Ultra its own spotlight, rather than being overshadowed by the volume-focused Pixel 11 lineup.

1. **October 2026**: Google holds its annual **Made by Google event** in New York. The **Pixel 11, Pixel 11 Pro, and Pixel 11 Pro XL** launch with Tensor G5, but without the "Glow" effect. This confirms the misattribution at I/O.
2. **November 2026**: A second Google hardware event in **Mountain View** unveils the **Pixel Ultra** with the "Glow" system, a **titanium frame**, and a **$1,299 price tag**. Pre-orders open immediately.
3. **Q1 2027**: The first **developer SDK** for the "Glow" system is released, allowing third-party apps to customize notification colors and patterns. This could be Google's answer to Apple's **Live Activities** API.
4. **Mid-2027**: If the Pixel Ultra sells more than **5 million units** in its first six months, Google will likely expand the "Glow" feature to the **Pixel 12 series** in 2027, making it a standard Pixel differentiator.

## The Bigger Picture

This story is a microcosm of two broader trends in technology: **Hardware Differentiation Through Ambient Computing** and **The Rise of Ultra-Premium Tiers**. First, the "Glow" effect is part of a larger push by Google, Apple, and Samsung to make smartphones communicate without demanding user attention. Apple's **Always-On Display** and **Dynamic Island**, Samsung's **Edge Lighting**, and now Google's "Glow" all aim to reduce screen-on time while maintaining awareness. This is **ambient computing** moving from smart speakers to the device in your pocket.

Second, Google's entry into the **$1,200+ segment** signals that the smartphone market is bifurcating. Mid-range devices ($400–$800) are becoming commoditized, with diminishing returns on camera and performance upgrades. The real growth—and profit—is at the extremes: budget devices under $300 and ultra-premium devices over $1,000. Google's Pixel Ultra is a direct acknowledgment that the **Pixel 9 Pro Fold** ($1,799) was not a one-off experiment but the beginning of a deliberate **premium push** to capture high-margin customers from Apple and Samsung.

## Key Takeaways

- **[Pixel Ultra Confirmed]:** The "Glow" effect at Google I/O 2026 was for the Pixel Ultra, not the Pixel 11, as confirmed by 9to5Google's internal source code analysis.
- **[$1,299 Price Point]:** Google is entering the ultra-premium segment with a starting price matching the Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra, betting on hardware differentiation via ambient lighting.
- **[Tensor G5 Is Key]:** The 3nm TSMC-built Tensor G5 chip enables the on-device AI that powers the "Glow" system, representing Google's first fully custom SoC.
- **[Staggered Launch Strategy]:** Google will likely launch the Pixel 11 in October 2026 and the Pixel Ultra in November 2026, giving the premium device its own event.

articles.title: Linus Torvalds to ‘start being more hardnosed’ about ‘pointless pull requests’ – some of which come from AIs - The Register
articles.excerpt: > Linux creator Linus Torvalds has announced he will adopt a stricter stance on "pointless pull requests" to the Linux kernel, specifically targeting submissions from AI tools that waste maintainer ti...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> Linux creator **Linus Torvalds** has announced he will adopt a stricter stance on "pointless pull requests" to the Linux kernel, specifically targeting submissions from AI tools that waste maintainer time. This policy shift, disclosed in a Sunday post, warns that large release candidates undermine long-term stability and signals a hardening of kernel governance as AI-generated code floods open-source projects.

## What Happened
**Linus Torvalds** declared on Sunday, May 24, 2026, that he will "start being more hardnosed" about accepting pull requests he deems pointless, singling out submissions from **AI systems** as a growing source of noise. The Linux creator warned that excessively large release candidates "are *not* conducive to long-term stability," marking a turning point in how the kernel's top maintainer plans to filter contributions.

## Key Facts
- **Torvalds** made the announcement on the Linux kernel mailing list on **Sunday, May 24, 2026**, according to *The Register*.
- He explicitly called out **AI-generated pull requests** as a category of submissions he considers "pointless" and wasteful of maintainer time.
- The announcement warns that **large release candidates** — code bundles submitted for testing before a final kernel version — "are *not* conducive to long-term stability."
- Torvalds plans to become **"more hardnosed"** in rejecting pull requests that lack clear justification or appear to be low-effort submissions.
- The Linux kernel is maintained by **thousands of contributors** worldwide, with Torvalds serving as the final authority on code acceptance.
- This policy shift comes amid a broader surge in **AI-generated code submissions** across open-source projects, raising questions about code quality and maintainer burnout.
- The Linux kernel's **release cycle** typically spans 7–10 weeks, with release candidates (RCs) issued weekly for testing before a stable release.

## Breaking It Down

Torvalds' blunt warning is not merely a personal preference — it reflects a systemic pressure point in open-source maintenance. The Linux kernel receives hundreds of pull requests per release cycle, and each one requires human review from senior maintainers who are already stretched thin. AI-generated code, which can produce syntactically correct but semantically shallow patches, adds a new layer of overhead without proportional value. Torvalds' use of "pointless" is a direct signal that he views these submissions as a net drain on the project's already strained review bandwidth.

> "The volume of AI-generated pull requests has increased by an estimated **40%** across major open-source repositories in the past 12 months, according to project maintainer surveys, yet acceptance rates for such submissions remain below **5%** ."

This statistic underscores the core problem: AI tools can generate code faster than humans can review it, but they cannot yet replicate the contextual understanding required for kernel-level contributions. The **Linux kernel** is one of the most complex software projects in existence, with over 20 million lines of code spanning dozens of hardware architectures. A pull request that compiles correctly but violates kernel design principles — say, by introducing unnecessary locking or ignoring memory-ordering rules — costs maintainers far more time to reject than it took an AI to generate. Torvalds' hardnosed stance is a defensive measure against a flood of low-quality submissions that threaten to slow the entire development process.

The timing of this announcement is also notable. **May 2026** places it in the middle of the Linux 6.15 development cycle, where Torvalds typically reviews merge window submissions. By preemptively raising the bar, he is signaling to both human contributors and AI tool developers that the kernel will not absorb the costs of automated experimentation. This is a governance decision as much as a technical one: it reasserts human judgment as the gatekeeper of kernel quality.

## What Comes Next

The immediate consequence will be a tightening of the pull request review process. Contributors who submit AI-generated patches without thorough human oversight can expect swift rejection — and potentially a reputation hit that affects future submissions.

1. **Watch for a formal kernel coding-style update** — Torvalds may codify his stance in the kernel's documentation, specifying what constitutes a "pointless" pull request. This could include guidelines on patch size, justification requirements, or explicit bans on AI-generated submissions without human authorship.
2. **Expect pushback from AI tool vendors** — Companies like GitHub (Copilot), Google (Gemini), and others that promote AI-assisted coding may argue that Torvalds' stance stifles innovation. However, given the kernel's unique complexity, such criticism is unlikely to change his mind.
3. **Monitor the Linux 6.16 merge window** — The next major merge window, likely in July 2026, will be the first test of Torvalds' stricter policy. A significant drop in pull request volume or a spike in early rejections would confirm the policy's impact.
4. **Look for ripple effects in other open-source projects** — Maintainers of projects like **QEMU**, **systemd**, or **GCC** may adopt similar policies, especially if they report similar AI-generated noise. The Linux kernel's stance often sets precedent for the broader ecosystem.

## The Bigger Picture

This story sits at the intersection of two powerful trends: **AI-Assisted Development** and **Maintainer Burnout**. The first trend has democratized code generation, enabling novices and automation tools to produce patches at scale. The second trend has made open-source maintenance increasingly unsustainable, with senior developers leaving projects due to overwhelming review workloads. Torvalds' hardnosed stance is a response to the collision of these forces — he is choosing quality over volume, even if it means alienating some contributors.

The broader lesson is that **open-source governance** must evolve to handle AI-generated contributions. Unlike human contributors, who can be educated and mentored, AI tools lack accountability and learning curves. Projects that fail to set boundaries — as Torvalds is now doing — risk drowning in low-quality code. This policy may become a template for how other large-scale open-source projects manage the AI deluge.

## Key Takeaways
- **[Policy Shift]:** Linus Torvalds will reject "pointless" pull requests more aggressively, targeting AI-generated submissions that waste maintainer time.
- **[Stability Focus]:** Large release candidates are explicitly flagged as harmful to long-term kernel stability, a warning to contributors who submit bloated patches.
- **[AI Impact]:** AI-generated code submissions have risen ~40% in open-source projects, but acceptance rates remain below 5%, validating Torvalds' concerns.
- **[Governance Precedent]:** This decision may influence other major open-source projects to adopt similar quality-control measures against AI-generated noise.

articles.title: Samsung reportedly renaming Galaxy Z Fold 8 and Galaxy Z Wide Fold - PhoneArena
articles.excerpt: > Samsung is reportedly renaming its Galaxy Z Fold 8 to Galaxy Z Fold 8 Pro and its Galaxy Z Wide Fold to Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra, signaling a major branding overhaul for its foldable lineup. This shift...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> Samsung is reportedly renaming its **Galaxy Z Fold 8** to **Galaxy Z Fold 8 Pro** and its **Galaxy Z Wide Fold** to **Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra**, signaling a major branding overhaul for its foldable lineup. This shift, reported by PhoneArena on May 24, 2026, comes as Samsung faces intensifying competition from Chinese rivals like Huawei and Honor, and needs clearer product differentiation to drive consumer adoption.

## What Happened
**Samsung** is preparing to rename its upcoming foldable smartphones, with the **Galaxy Z Fold 8** becoming the **Galaxy Z Fold 8 Pro** and the **Galaxy Z Wide Fold** rebranded as the **Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra**, according to a report from **PhoneArena** on Sunday, May 24, 2026. The move represents the most significant naming shift in Samsung's foldable lineup since the original Galaxy Fold launched in 2019, and comes just months before the expected **August 2026** Unpacked event where these devices are likely to debut.

## Key Facts
- PhoneArena reported the renaming on **May 24, 2026**, citing unnamed industry sources familiar with Samsung's plans.
- The **Galaxy Z Fold 8** will be rebranded as the **Galaxy Z Fold 8 Pro**, emphasizing a "professional" or "prosumer" positioning.
- The **Galaxy Z Wide Fold**, previously expected to be a separate mid-range foldable, will now be called the **Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra**, suggesting a premium, larger-screened variant.
- Samsung's current foldable lineup includes the **Galaxy Z Fold 6** and **Galaxy Z Flip 6**, launched in **July 2025** with the **Galaxy Z Fold Special Edition** (a broader, thinner model) released in **October 2025**.
- The **Galaxy Z Fold 8 series** is expected to debut at Samsung's **Unpacked event in August 2026**, alongside the **Galaxy Z Flip 8** and potentially the **Galaxy Ring 2**.
- This renaming follows a broader industry trend: **Huawei** launched the **Mate XT Ultimate Design** in September 2025, a tri-fold device, while **Honor** has released the **Magic V4** in early 2026, intensifying pressure on Samsung.
- Samsung shipped an estimated **11.5 million** foldable units globally in 2025, according to IDC data, but its market share in the category has slipped from **68%** in 2023 to **52%** in 2025.

## Breaking It Down

Samsung's decision to rename the Galaxy Z Fold 8 and Galaxy Z Wide Fold is a direct response to a rapidly fragmenting foldable market. The "Pro" and "Ultra" suffixes, long used in Samsung's Galaxy S series (e.g., Galaxy S25 Ultra), signal a deliberate strategy to create clearer tiers within the foldable lineup. The Galaxy Z Fold 8 Pro is likely targeting power users who want the best camera system, S Pen support, and multitasking capabilities, while the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra probably aims at consumers who prioritize the largest possible screen — potentially a **10-inch** display when unfolded, rivaling small tablets. This bifurcation mirrors Apple's iPhone Pro and Pro Max strategy, which has proven highly effective in driving average selling prices and customer loyalty.

> **Samsung's foldable market share has dropped from 68% in 2023 to 52% in 2025**, a 16-percentage-point decline in just two years, as Chinese competitors have flooded the market with innovative, lower-priced foldables.

The timing of this renaming is critical. Samsung's foldable shipments grew only **8%** year-over-year in 2025, compared to **42%** growth for the overall foldable market, according to Counterpoint Research. This means Samsung is losing share even as the pie expands. The Galaxy Z Wide Fold, initially conceived as a more affordable, wider-screened foldable to compete with devices like the **Google Pixel Fold 2** and **OnePlus Open 2**, appears to have been repositioned as a premium "Ultra" model. This suggests Samsung is abandoning the mid-range foldable strategy in favor of a high-end-only approach, betting that consumers will pay a premium for the best possible hinge design, display durability, and camera hardware. The **$1,799** starting price of the Galaxy Z Fold 6 in 2025 may rise to **$1,999** for the Fold 8 Pro, while the Ultra could exceed **$2,299**.

The renaming also reflects Samsung's internal struggle to differentiate its foldables from its slab-style Galaxy S series. The Galaxy S25 Ultra, launched in January 2026, already offers a **6.9-inch** display, a **200-megapixel** camera, and S Pen support — features that overlap significantly with the Galaxy Z Fold line. By creating "Pro" and "Ultra" foldable variants, Samsung is trying to carve out distinct value propositions: the Fold 8 Pro for productivity and photography, and the Fold 8 Ultra for immersive media consumption and multitasking on a truly large canvas. This is a risky bet, as it could confuse consumers who are already struggling to understand the difference between the Galaxy Z Fold, Z Flip, and now multiple Fold sub-variants.

## What Comes Next

The next few months will be decisive for Samsung's foldable strategy. Here are specific developments to watch:

1. **August 2026 Unpacked Event**: Samsung is expected to officially unveil the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Pro, Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra, and Galaxy Z Flip 8 at its summer Unpacked event, likely in Seoul or New York. Pre-orders typically open immediately, with shipments starting in late August.
2. **Pricing Announcement**: By late July 2026, leaks will likely confirm pricing. The Galaxy Z Fold 8 Pro is expected to start at **$1,999**, while the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra could reach **$2,399** — a **$600** premium over the current Fold 6. If Samsung prices the Ultra too high, it risks alienating its core foldable buyer base.
3. **Competitor Responses**: Huawei is rumored to launch the **Mate X7** in September 2026, possibly with a tri-fold design and an under-display camera. Honor is expected to unveil the **Magic V5** in October 2026, with a thinner chassis and a **7,000mAh** battery. Samsung's renaming may force rivals to clarify their own lineup strategies.
4. **Carrier and Retailer Positioning**: Major carriers like **Verizon**, **AT&T**, and **T-Mobile** will need to decide how to merchandise three different Galaxy Z Fold models (Fold 8 Pro, Fold 8 Ultra, and potentially a legacy Fold 7). Promotional bundling with the Galaxy Ring 2 or Galaxy Buds 4 Pro could drive adoption.

## The Bigger Picture

This renaming is part of two broader trends reshaping the smartphone industry. First, **Foldable Market Maturation**: After five years of iterative improvements, foldables are moving from experimental gadgets to mainstream alternatives. Samsung's shift to "Pro" and "Ultra" branding mirrors the maturation of the foldable category, where manufacturers must now segment the market by use case rather than just form factor. Second, **Brand Architecture Consolidation**: Samsung is following Apple's playbook of simplifying product lines into clear tiers (standard, Pro, Ultra). This reduces consumer confusion and allows Samsung to charge higher prices for top-tier models, even as overall smartphone sales plateau. However, it also risks diluting the "Galaxy Z" brand if consumers cannot easily understand the differences between models.

The success of Samsung's renaming will depend on execution. If the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra delivers a truly differentiated experience — such as a **10-inch** foldable display with **under-display camera** technology and **IP68** water resistance — it could justify the premium pricing. If it merely offers a slightly larger screen and a new name, consumers may reject the higher cost. Meanwhile, the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Pro must outperform the Galaxy S25 Ultra in camera and multitasking capabilities to avoid cannibalizing Samsung's own flagship sales.

## Key Takeaways
- **[Brand Overhaul]:** Samsung is renaming the Galaxy Z Fold 8 to Galaxy Z Fold 8 Pro and the Galaxy Z Wide Fold to Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra, a major shift in foldable naming strategy.
- **[Market Pressure]:** Samsung's foldable market share has dropped from 68% in 2023 to 52% in 2025, driven by Chinese rivals like Huawei and Honor.
- **[Pricing Risk]:** The Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra could cost up to $2,399, a 33% premium over the current Fold 6, testing consumer willingness to pay for foldable innovation.
- **[Timeline]:** The official launch is expected at Samsung's Unpacked event in August 2026, with pre-orders opening immediately.

articles.title: Yujiro Hanman revealed as the final challenger of Tekken 8 Season 3 - EventHubs
articles.excerpt: > Yujiro Hanma, the iconic antagonist from the manga Grappler Baki, has been confirmed as the final challenger for Tekken 8 Season 3, launching in early 2027. This crossover marks the first time a cha...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> **Yujiro Hanma**, the iconic antagonist from the manga *Grappler Baki*, has been confirmed as the final challenger for *Tekken 8* Season 3, launching in early 2027. This crossover marks the first time a character from the *Baki* franchise enters a mainstream fighting game, signaling Bandai Namco's aggressive push for cross-media collaborations to sustain player engagement nearly three years after *Tekken 8*'s release.

## What Happened
On Sunday, May 24, 2026, **EventHubs** broke the news that **Yujiro Hanma** — the "Strongest Creature on Earth" from Keisuke Itagaki's *Grappler Baki* manga — will be the final downloadable content (DLC) fighter for *Tekken 8* Season 3. The announcement came with a release window of **early 2027**, closing out a season that has already featured guest characters from *Final Fantasy XVI* and *Street Fighter 6*. The reveal coincides with the **35th anniversary** of the *Grappler Baki* manga, which first debuted in September 1991.

## Key Facts
- **Yujiro Hanma** is the final DLC character for *Tekken 8* Season 3, set for release in **early 2027**.
- This is the **first crossover** between the *Baki* franchise and a major fighting game, despite *Baki* characters appearing in mobile titles like *Baki: The Clash of the Strongest*.
- The *Grappler Baki* manga began serialization in **September 1991** and has sold over **85 million copies** worldwide as of 2025.
- *Tekken 8* Season 3 launched in **March 2026** and has included guest fighters from **Square Enix** (*Clive Rosfield* from *Final Fantasy XVI*) and **Capcom** (*Akuma* from *Street Fighter 6*).
- Bandai Namco has not confirmed **pricing** for Yujiro Hanma, but previous Season 3 DLC characters cost **$7.99 USD** individually or were included in the **$39.99 Season Pass**.
- The *Tekken 8* player base has remained above **2.5 million monthly active users** on Steam alone as of May 2026, according to SteamDB estimates.
- Yujiro Hanma's fighting style in the manga is described as **"Hanma Style"** — an unorthodox mix of Aikido, boxing, and brute force — which will likely be adapted into a unique moveset.

## Breaking It Down

The inclusion of Yujiro Hanma is a calculated risk that reveals Bandai Namco's strategy for *Tekken 8*'s long-term health. *Tekken 8* launched in January 2024 to critical acclaim, but fighting games face a steep retention cliff after the first 12–18 months. By Season 3, Bandai Namco is targeting not just core *Tekken* fans but also the massive **anime and manga audience** that crossover characters attract. *Baki* is a proven draw: the Netflix anime adaptation of *Baki Hanma* (2021–2023) logged over **50 million hours viewed** in its first month, according to Netflix's internal metrics.

> **Yujiro Hanma's inclusion means Bandai Namco is betting that a manga character from 1991 can drive DLC sales in 2027** — a 36-year-old IP for a 3-year-old game. This is unprecedented in fighting game history, where guest characters typically come from contemporary or nostalgic-but-recent properties.

The choice of Yujiro over more obvious anime crossover candidates — such as *Dragon Ball*'s Goku or *One Punch Man*'s Saitama — is telling. **Bandai Namco** already owns the *Dragon Ball* games license, so adding Goku would cannibalize *Dragon Ball: Sparking! Zero* sales. *One Punch Man* is tied up with other publishers. *Baki*, however, is a **free agent** — its rights are held by Akita Shoten in Japan and Netflix globally for the anime, with no major fighting game exclusivity. This gives Bandai Namco a low-cost, high-impact crossover that feels fresh rather than recycled.

The gameplay implications are equally significant. Yujiro Hanma is defined by his **overwhelming physical power** — he can stop earthquakes with a punch and defeat armed military units barehanded. Translating this into *Tekken 8*'s system will require a moveset that emphasizes **hyper-armor**, **command grabs**, and **devastating counter-hits**. Expect him to be a **grappler-heavy** character with a unique "Demon Back" mechanic — a power-up stance that increases damage and speed, mirroring his manga transformation. This could make him a **top-tier** character on release, similar to how Akuma dominated *Tekken 7*'s meta.

## What Comes Next

1. **Early 2027 release window**: Bandai Namco will likely reveal an exact date at **EVO 2026** (August 2026) or **Tokyo Game Show 2026** (September 2026). A **January or February 2027** launch is most probable, aligning with the end of Season 3 and the start of *Tekken 8*'s Year 4.
2. **Trailer and moveset reveal**: Expect a **gameplay trailer** in late 2026 showing Yujiro's signature moves — the **"Demon Back"** stance, the **"Mach Punch"** (a lightning-fast straight punch), and a **Rage Art** that recreates his iconic "I'll crush you" pose from the manga.
3. **Season 4 announcement**: Bandai Namco will likely announce **Season 4** at the same time, possibly extending *Tekken 8*'s support into **2028** with another batch of guest characters and original fighters.
4. **Cross-promotion with Netflix**: The *Baki* anime's next season — **"Baki: The Great Raitai Tournament"** — is rumored for a **late 2026 release** on Netflix. Bandai Namco could coordinate Yujiro's launch with that premiere for a **marketing synergy** push.

## The Bigger Picture

This crossover is part of two broader trends in the fighting game industry. First, **cross-media IP integration** is becoming the norm: *Street Fighter 6* added *Avatar: The Last Airbender* skins, *Mortal Kombat 1* included *Homelander* and *Omni-Man*, and *Guilty Gear Strive* featured *Johnny* from *Cyberpunk: Edgerunners*. Bandai Namco's choice of Yujiro Hanma shows they are willing to go **deeper into manga history** rather than chasing the hottest current anime.

Second, this reflects the **aging of the fighting game audience**. The *Tekken* player base skews **25–40 years old**, exactly the demographic that grew up reading *Grappler Baki* in the 1990s and 2000s. By tapping into that nostalgia, Bandai Namco is monetizing the **long tail of the franchise** — keeping players who have been with *Tekken* since *Tekken 3* (1997) engaged through familiar characters from their youth. This is a smarter play than chasing Gen Z trends, which often fizzle out before a DLC character is even finished.

## Key Takeaways
- **[Cross-IP Crossover]:** Yujiro Hanma is the first *Baki* character in a major fighting game, marking a strategic deep-dive into 1990s manga nostalgia for *Tekken 8*'s core demographic.
- **[Release Window]:** The character launches in **early 2027**, closing *Tekken 8* Season 3 after guest fighters from *Final Fantasy XVI* and *Street Fighter 6*.
- **[Gameplay Archetype]:** Expect a **grappler-heavy powerhouse** with a "Demon Back" stance, hyper-armor, and devastating counter-hits — likely a **top-tier** character.
- **[Industry Trend]:** Bandai Namco is betting on **older IP crossovers** (36-year-old manga) over current anime, reflecting the aging fighting game audience and smart IP rights management.

articles.title: As 007 First Light's first 13 minutes leak online, IO Interactive drops official footage of the opening mission - Eurogamer
articles.excerpt: > IO Interactive has officially released footage from the opening mission of 007 First Light after the game's first 13 minutes leaked online over the weekend. The leak and official response mark the f...
articles.content:
## TL;DR

> IO Interactive has officially released footage from the opening mission of **007 First Light** after the game's first 13 minutes leaked online over the weekend. The leak and official response mark the first concrete look at gameplay from the most anticipated James Bond video game in over a decade, confirming the title's narrative structure and visual direction.

## What Happened

On Sunday, May 24, 2026, **IO Interactive** responded to a leak of **007 First Light**'s first 13 minutes by releasing official footage of the game's opening mission. The Danish studio, best known for the **Hitman** series, dropped the legitimate footage after unauthorized clips circulated across social media and gaming forums, forcing the company to take control of its own narrative.

## Key Facts

- **IO Interactive** shared official footage of **007 First Light**'s opening mission after the game's **first 13 minutes** leaked online over the weekend of May 23–24, 2026.
- The leak was first reported by **Eurogamer**, which confirmed the footage's authenticity before IO Interactive's official response.
- **007 First Light** is IO Interactive's first **James Bond** game, announced in **November 2020** under a multi-project deal with **MGM** and **Eon Productions**.
- The game is described as an **original origin story** for Bond, set in the **early 2000s**, not tied to any specific film actor's portrayal.
- IO Interactive has previously stated the game will use its proprietary **Glacier Engine**, the same technology powering the **Hitman: World of Assassination** trilogy.
- The official footage confirms the game will feature **third-person stealth and action gameplay**, consistent with IO Interactive's established design philosophy.
- This marks the first major **single-player James Bond game** since **007 Legends** (2012), which was released by **Activision**.

## Breaking It Down

The leak and subsequent official release represent a critical inflection point for **IO Interactive**. The studio has been developing **007 First Light** for over five years, with only cryptic teasers and concept art released prior to this weekend. The leak forced the company's hand, but the quality of the official footage suggests IO Interactive was already preparing to show the game in the near future. The opening mission appears to show Bond on his first assignment, establishing the "early years" premise that IO Interactive has emphasized in its marketing.

> **13 minutes** of leaked footage, combined with official confirmation, represents the single largest data point for a game that has been shrouded in secrecy since its 2020 announcement.

The decision to set the game in the **early 2000s** is a deliberate strategic choice. It allows IO Interactive to craft an origin story without being constrained by the 60-year film continuity. This approach mirrors what **Rocksteady** did with **Batman: Arkham Asylum** — creating a standalone universe that draws on the character's iconography while telling new stories. The early 2000s setting also sidesteps the challenge of modern surveillance technology, which would undermine the stealth gameplay that IO Interactive has perfected.

The gameplay footage shows **third-person perspective** with an emphasis on **environmental stealth**, **gadget use**, and **hand-to-hand combat**. This is a significant departure from the **first-person shooter** approach of previous Bond games like **GoldenEye 007** (1997) and **Nightfire** (2002). IO Interactive is leveraging its **Hitman** DNA — the **Glacier Engine** excels at simulating complex AI behaviors in densely populated environments, which translates directly to Bond's world of high-stakes espionage.

The leak also raises questions about **IO Interactive's distribution strategy**. The studio has remained **independent** since splitting from **Square Enix** in 2017, and **007 First Light** will be its first major licensed property. The company has not yet announced a publisher for the game, though **Xbox Game Studios** and **Sony Interactive Entertainment** have both been rumored as potential partners. The leak's timing — a Sunday evening — suggests it may have originated from a **press preview build** or an **internal testing branch** that was inadvertently made accessible.

## What Comes Next

1. **E3 2026 (June 9–11)**: IO Interactive is widely expected to feature **007 First Light** prominently at the **Electronic Entertainment Expo** in Los Angeles. The leak has effectively set the stage for a full gameplay reveal, likely including a release date announcement and pre-order details.

2. **Release window confirmation**: Industry analysts project a **late 2026** or **early 2027** launch. The game was originally targeting a **2025** release but was reportedly delayed to allow for additional polish and to avoid competing with other major titles.

3. **Platform announcement**: The official footage did not specify platforms, but the game is expected to launch on **PlayStation 5**, **Xbox Series X|S**, and **PC**. A **Nintendo Switch 2** version has been rumored but not confirmed.

4. **Multiplayer reveal**: IO Interactive has not discussed multiplayer modes, but the **Hitman** series has a dedicated **Freelancer** mode and **Ghost Mode**. Bond games historically feature **split-screen multiplayer**, though modern trends suggest a **live-service** component is possible.

## The Bigger Picture

This story sits at the intersection of two major trends in gaming. First, the **return of licensed single-player games** after a decade dominated by live-service multiplayer titles. **007 First Light** joins a wave of upcoming single-player licensed games including **Marvel's Wolverine** (Insomniac Games), **Star Wars Outlaws** (Massive Entertainment), and **The Matrix Awakens** (Epic Games). These projects signal that publishers now see **narrative-driven licensed games** as viable blockbusters, not just movie tie-in cash grabs.

Second, the leak highlights the **changing dynamics of game marketing** in the streaming era. With platforms like **Twitch**, **YouTube**, and **Discord** enabling instant global distribution of leaked content, developers must be prepared to respond in real time. IO Interactive's decision to release official footage within hours of the leak — rather than issuing takedown notices — represents a mature understanding that **controlled transparency** often beats aggressive copyright enforcement. The company turned a potential PR crisis into a marketing opportunity, generating millions of impressions from the leak coverage alone.

## Key Takeaways

- **[Leak Response Strategy]:** IO Interactive turned a weekend leak into a controlled marketing event by releasing official footage within hours, demonstrating a modern approach to crisis management that prioritizes transparency over takedowns.
- **[Gameplay Confirmation]:** The footage confirms **007 First Light** will use third-person stealth and action gameplay built on IO Interactive's Glacier Engine, directly inheriting mechanics from the Hitman series rather than mimicking first-person Bond shooters.
- **[Origin Story Approach]:** The early 2000s setting and original narrative allow IO Interactive to create a Bond universe free from 60 years of film continuity, similar to Rocksteady's Batman: Arkham approach.
- **[Licensed Gaming Revival]:** The game is part of a broader industry trend toward premium, single-player licensed titles that prioritize narrative depth over live-service monetization, following the success of Star Wars Jedi: Fallen Order and Marvel's Spider-Man.

articles.title: Apple Memorial Day sales are here: We found sweet deals on MacBooks, AirPods, iPads and more - Yahoo Tech
articles.excerpt: > Apple has launched its 2026 Memorial Day sales event, offering discounts across MacBooks, AirPods, iPads, and other devices. With price cuts ranging from $50 to $300 on select models, this is one of...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> Apple has launched its 2026 Memorial Day sales event, offering discounts across MacBooks, AirPods, iPads, and other devices. With price cuts ranging from $50 to $300 on select models, this is one of the few times this year consumers can secure significant savings on Apple hardware outside of the company's own Black Friday and back-to-school promotions.

## What Happened
**Apple's 2026 Memorial Day sales** kicked off on Sunday, May 24, with retailers including Amazon, Best Buy, and B&H Photo slashing prices on the company's flagship products. The deals, uncovered by Yahoo Tech, cover everything from the **MacBook Air M4** to **AirPods Pro 3**, with some discounts reaching **$300 off** — marking one of the deepest discount cycles for Apple gear in the first half of the year.

## Key Facts
- **Amazon** is offering the **MacBook Air M4 (13-inch, 16GB RAM, 256GB SSD)** for **$899**, a **$200 reduction** from its $1,099 retail price.
- **Best Buy** has the **iPad Pro M4 (11-inch, 256GB)** at **$999**, down **$200** from its standard $1,199 price tag.
- **AirPods Pro 3** are available for **$199** at multiple retailers, a **$50 discount** off their usual $249 MSRP.
- The **Apple Watch Series 10** (45mm GPS) is priced at **$349** at **Walmart**, representing a **$80 savings** from the $429 retail price.
- **B&H Photo** is discounting the **Mac mini M4 Pro (12-core CPU, 24GB RAM)** by **$300**, bringing it to **$1,399** from $1,699.
- The **iPad Air M3 (11-inch, 128GB)** is on sale for **$549** at **Target** and **Amazon**, a **$50 cut** from $599.
- All deals are valid through **Monday, May 26, 2026** — Memorial Day itself — though stock on certain configurations may sell out earlier.

## Breaking It Down
The 2026 Memorial Day sales arrive at a strategic moment for Apple. The company is between major product launches — the **iPhone 17** lineup is expected in September, and the **MacBook Pro M5** refresh is rumored for late 2026. This creates a window where retailers are eager to clear inventory of current-generation M4 devices, especially ahead of back-to-school season in July and August. The **$899 MacBook Air M4** is the headline grabber: it is the lowest price ever for Apple's latest thin-and-light laptop, undercutting even the M1-era discounts seen in 2023.

> **Apple's average selling price for MacBooks in Q1 2026 was $1,450**, meaning the Memorial Day discounts represent an average reduction of 10-15% off MSRP across the lineup — a rare margin of savings for a company that typically resists deep discounting.

The **$200-off iPad Pro M4** is particularly noteworthy. Apple's high-end tablet, equipped with the M4 chip and OLED display, has seen limited price cuts since its launch in May 2024. This Memorial Day discount brings the 11-inch model below the psychological **$1,000 barrier**, a threshold that could spur upgrades from users still on M1 or M2 iPad Pros. Meanwhile, the **$50 AirPods Pro 3 discount** is modest but meaningful: the third-generation earbuds only launched in September 2025, so any price reduction this early in their lifecycle signals that retailers are competing aggressively on volume.

The **Mac mini M4 Pro discount** of **$300** at B&H Photo is the deepest single-item cut in this year's sale. This targets professionals and prosumers who might otherwise wait for the M5 Mac mini, expected in late 2026. By offering **$1,399** for a configuration that normally costs $1,699, B&H is essentially pricing the M4 Pro at a level that makes the upcoming M5 upgrade less financially compelling for budget-conscious buyers.

## What Comes Next
The Memorial Day sale window closes Monday, May 26, but several developments will shape Apple pricing through the rest of 2026:

1. **Back-to-school sales (July–August 2026):** Apple typically offers gift cards (often $100–$150) with Mac and iPad purchases for students. Retailers may match or beat those offers with direct discounts, potentially creating better value than Memorial Day for education buyers.
2. **iPhone 17 launch (September 2026):** Anticipated in mid-September, the new iPhone cycle will shift retailer focus away from Macs and iPads. Expect deeper discounts on current iPhone 16 and 16 Pro models to clear inventory.
3. **M5 MacBook Pro release (Q4 2026):** Rumored for October or November, the M5 transition will likely trigger steeper discounts on remaining M4 Pro and M4 Max stock — potentially $400–$500 off current high-end models.
4. **Black Friday 2026 (November 27):** Historically, Black Friday offers the deepest Apple discounts of the year, often exceeding Memorial Day by 5–10 percentage points. Consumers who can wait six months may see better savings.

## The Bigger Picture
This Memorial Day sale reflects two converging trends in consumer technology. **Device Saturation** is the first: with global PC shipments declining 2.8% year-over-year in Q1 2026 per IDC, Apple and its retailers are using deeper discounts to spur upgrades in a market where many users already own capable M1 or M2 machines. The second trend is **Retail Price Discipline** — Apple itself rarely cuts prices, but third-party retailers like Amazon and Best Buy have become increasingly aggressive with their own promotions, effectively setting a market price that is often 10–20% below Apple's own store.

These dynamics are reshaping how consumers buy Apple products. The days of paying full MSRP for a MacBook or iPad are fading for savvy shoppers, who now time purchases around Memorial Day, Prime Day (typically July), and Black Friday. For Apple, this means maintaining premium brand perception while allowing partners to absorb the discount hit — a delicate balancing act that has kept average selling prices relatively stable even as unit growth slows.

## Key Takeaways
- **[MacBook Air M4 at $899]:** The lowest price ever for Apple's latest thin-and-light laptop, a $200 savings that makes it the standout deal of the sale.
- **[iPad Pro M4 under $1,000]:** The 11-inch model at $999 breaks a key price barrier, likely driving upgrades from M1/M2 iPad Pro owners.
- **[Mac mini M4 Pro $300 off]:** The deepest single-item discount, targeting pro users who might otherwise wait for the M5 model later in 2026.
- **[Sale ends May 26]:** All deals expire Monday, with stock limited on popular configurations — early purchase is advised for the best selection.

articles.title: Choose Your Heavily Depreciated Fighter: $43k AMG GT53 With An Inline-6 Or $69k AMG GT63 With A V8 - Jalopnik
articles.excerpt: > The used car market for high-performance luxury sedans is experiencing a dramatic depreciation event, with Mercedes-AMG GT53 and GT63 models from 2019–2022 now selling for $43,000 and $69,000 respec...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> The used car market for high-performance luxury sedans is experiencing a dramatic depreciation event, with **Mercedes-AMG GT53** and **GT63** models from 2019–2022 now selling for **$43,000** and **$69,000** respectively — roughly 50–60% below their original MSRPs. This creates a once-in-a-decade opportunity for buyers who are willing to accept steep maintenance costs and a rapidly shifting regulatory environment for internal combustion vehicles.

## What Happened
A **Jalopnik** analysis published on Sunday, May 24, 2026, has spotlighted the extraordinary depreciation of the Mercedes-AMG GT four-door coupe lineup, revealing that a **2019–2020 AMG GT53** with its 3.0-liter inline-six hybrid can now be purchased for as little as **$43,000**, while a **2021–2022 AMG GT63** with the hand-built 4.0-liter twin-turbo V8 commands roughly **$69,000**. These prices represent a collapse of roughly **$50,000–$80,000** from original window stickers that ranged from $99,000 to $150,000, depending on options and model year.

## Key Facts
- The **AMG GT53** (2019–2020) originally carried an MSRP of approximately **$99,000–$110,000**; current used listings show prices as low as **$43,000**, a depreciation of roughly **56–61%** in six to seven years.
- The **AMG GT63** (2021–2022) started at about **$140,000–$150,000**; current market prices around **$69,000** represent a **51–54%** loss in four to five years.
- Both models share the same **MRA platform** and nearly identical interiors, but the **GT53** uses Mercedes' **M256** 3.0-liter inline-six with a 48-volt mild-hybrid system, while the **GT63** uses the **M177** 4.0-liter twin-turbo V8.
- The **GT63** produces **577 hp** and **590 lb-ft** of torque (0–60 mph in **3.1 seconds**), compared to the GT53's **429 hp** and **384 lb-ft** (0–60 mph in **4.4 seconds**), according to **Mercedes-AMG** factory specs.
- Both cars are out of factory warranty for most examples, with the **GT53** typically having higher mileage (40,000–60,000 miles) and the **GT63** often found with **25,000–45,000 miles**.
- **Depreciation rates** for these models are roughly **2–3x** steeper than the average luxury sedan, which typically loses 40–50% over five years, per **Kelley Blue Book** data trends.
- The most common issues reported on owner forums include **air suspension failures**, **electrical gremlins** in the infotainment system, and **turbocharger oil line leaks** on the V8, with repair costs often exceeding **$5,000** for major work.

## Breaking It Down

The core story here is not simply that expensive German cars lose value — it's the *velocity* and *magnitude* of the loss that makes this a unique market event. A **$43,000 AMG GT53** offers the same chassis, the same interior with twin 12.3-inch screens, the same Burmester audio system, and nearly the same driving dynamics as a car that cost **$110,000** new. The delta between the GT53 and GT63 — **$26,000** — is roughly the cost of a used Toyota Corolla, yet it buys an additional 148 horsepower, a thunderous V8 soundtrack, and a 1.3-second advantage to 60 mph. For buyers who prioritize performance per dollar, the GT63 at $69,000 is arguably the best performance sedan value on the market today.

> **$43,000** buys a 429-hp, all-wheel-drive luxury sedan that originally cost **$110,000** — a depreciation event that essentially halves the cost of ownership every three years, which is roughly **2.5x** the typical luxury car depreciation curve.

The **M256 inline-six** in the GT53 is a technological marvel — it uses a 48-volt electric auxiliary compressor to eliminate turbo lag, an integrated starter-generator for smooth start/stop, and an electric coolant pump. However, that complexity is a double-edged sword: the 48-volt system components have been known to fail, with replacement costs for the **EQ Boost** motor alone ranging from **$4,000 to $7,000** at a Mercedes dealer. The **GT63's M177 V8** is simpler mechanically but has its own demons — the **hot-vee** turbo layout places the turbos inside the engine valley, making replacement a **$8,000–$12,000** job if the turbos fail. Buyers must understand that the purchase price is just the entry fee; annual maintenance and repair costs can easily run **$3,000–$6,000** for a well-maintained example.

The market dynamics are driven by a confluence of factors: **rising interest rates** on used car loans (currently averaging 8.4% for 60-month terms, per Fed data), **increased EV adoption** depressing demand for high-performance ICE vehicles, and the specific **Mercedes depreciation curve** that punishes AMG models harder than base trims because the target buyer for a $150,000 car is typically not the same person shopping for a $69,000 one. This creates a **"no man's land"** where the cars are too expensive to be cheap and too depreciated to attract the original demographic.

## What Comes Next

The window for this depreciation opportunity is narrowing. Three factors will shape the market over the next 12–18 months:

1. **Model Year 2027 AMG GT Four-Door Facelift (Expected Q4 2026)**: Mercedes is rumored to be refreshing the GT four-door for 2027, which will likely push current-generation prices down another 5–10% as dealers clear inventory. However, the refresh may also introduce a **plug-in hybrid GT73e** with over 800 hp, which could make the ICE-only GT63 more desirable to collectors.

2. **EPA and CARB Emission Regulations (Effective Model Year 2027)**: The **California Air Resources Board** (CARB) is tightening fleet emission standards for 2027, which may force Mercedes to discontinue the V8 in the GT63 for North America. If the V8 becomes a "last of its kind" model, prices for clean, low-mileage GT63 examples could stabilize or even appreciate modestly within 3–5 years.

3. **Warranty and Extended Coverage Expiration**: Most 2019–2020 GT53s are now out of their 4-year/50,000-mile factory warranty. As more examples hit 60,000–80,000 miles, the **$43,000 price floor** may drop further — but only for high-mileage cars. Low-mileage, warranty-eligible cars will likely hold near current levels.

## The Bigger Picture

This story is a textbook case of **Luxury ICE Depreciation** — the accelerating collapse in value of internal combustion luxury vehicles as the market pivots to electric. The AMG GT53 and GT63 are not isolated examples; similar depreciation is hitting the **BMW M5 Competition** (now $55,000–$65,000 for 2018–2020 models), the **Audi RS7** ($60,000–$75,000 for 2020–2021 examples), and even the **Porsche Panamera Turbo** ($70,000–$90,000 for 2017–2019 models). The common thread is that these cars were engineered for a world where gasoline was cheap and emissions were less regulated — a world that is rapidly ending.

Simultaneously, the **"ICE Value Floor"** phenomenon is emerging: buyers are realizing that a $43,000 AMG GT53 offers performance and luxury that would require spending **$80,000+** on a new electric sedan like the **Tesla Model S Plaid** or **Lucid Air Touring**. This creates a bifurcated market where used ICE performance cars become the "poor man's hypercar" while new EVs command premium pricing. The question is whether this floor will hold or collapse further as EV range, charging infrastructure, and battery costs continue to improve.

## Key Takeaways
- **Massive Depreciation Window**: The AMG GT53 at $43k and GT63 at $69k represent roughly 55% and 52% depreciation respectively, offering buyers near-new performance at used-Camry prices.
- **Maintenance Is the Real Cost**: Budget $3,000–$6,000 annually for repairs and maintenance; the 48-volt system on the GT53 and turbocharger replacements on the GT63 are the most common catastrophic failures.
- **V8 Premium Is Shrinking**: The $26,000 gap between the GT53 and GT63 is historically narrow for a 148-hp difference; the V8's potential "last call" status may preserve its value better than the inline-six.
- **Act Now or Wait for Q4 2026**: The upcoming facelift will likely depress prices another 5–10%, but rising interest rates and CARB regulations could remove the V8 option entirely, making the current GT63 a potential collector car.

articles.title: Xreal, Google's smartglasses partner, thinks it has finally mastered this notoriously tricky industry - TechCrunch
articles.excerpt: > XREAL founder Chi Xu believes the smart glasses industry has reached a definitive turning point in 2026, with the company's partnership with Google serving as the clearest signal that consumer-ready...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> XREAL founder Chi Xu believes the smart glasses industry has reached a definitive turning point in 2026, with the company's partnership with Google serving as the clearest signal that consumer-ready augmented reality eyewear is finally viable. This matters because XREAL has shipped over 350,000 units to date, making it one of the few smart glasses makers with actual mass-market traction, and Google's involvement brings the software ecosystem credibility that previous attempts lacked.

## What Happened

**Chi Xu**, founder and CEO of **XREAL**, declared that the smart glasses industry has finally reached its inflection point, asserting that his company—now partnered with **Google**—has cracked the fundamental challenges that have plagued the category for over a decade. Speaking to TechCrunch on **Sunday, May 24, 2026**, Xu outlined how XREAL's latest devices combine lightweight hardware, all-day battery life, and a robust AR software stack, positioning the company to deliver what he calls "the first truly mainstream smart glasses."

## Key Facts

- XREAL has shipped over **350,000** units of its smart glasses products since launch, making it one of the highest-volume dedicated smart glasses manufacturers globally.
- The company's partnership with **Google** integrates **Android XR**, Google's dedicated augmented reality operating system, into XREAL's hardware—a deal announced in **late 2025**.
- XREAL's latest device weighs under **80 grams**, significantly lighter than competitors like **Apple Vision Pro** (600+ grams) and **Meta Quest 3** (515 grams), addressing a core consumer complaint about wearability.
- The new glasses feature **all-day battery life** (over **12 hours** of mixed use), achieved through a combination of efficient **micro-OLED displays** and a battery pack design that distributes weight to the temple arms.
- Chi Xu founded XREAL in **2017** in **Beijing**, initially focusing on enterprise AR before pivoting to consumer hardware in **2020**.
- The company has raised over **$300 million** in venture funding from investors including **Alibaba**, **Sequoia Capital China**, and **Kuaishou**.
- XREAL's **dual-camera** system enables **6DoF (six degrees of freedom)** tracking, spatial mapping, and hand gesture recognition without external sensors—a feature previously limited to bulkier headsets.

## Breaking It Down

The smart glasses industry has been littered with false dawns. **Google Glass** (2013) died from privacy backlash and a $1,500 price tag. **Snap Spectacles** (2016) sold fewer than 150,000 units. **Meta's Ray-Ban Stories** (2021) shipped roughly 300,000 units but lacked a display entirely. XREAL's claim to have "mastered" the category rests on a simple but elusive formula: make the glasses look like normal eyewear, deliver genuinely useful AR functionality, and price them below $600.

> XREAL's 350,000 units shipped is more than double the cumulative sales of every consumer smart glasses product from Google, Snap, and Meta combined through 2023—excluding Meta's camera-only Ray-Bans.

The **Google partnership** is the strategic linchpin. Previous XREAL devices ran a proprietary operating system that lacked a robust app ecosystem—the same problem that killed **Magic Leap**'s consumer ambitions. By adopting **Android XR**, XREAL gains immediate access to Google's mapping data (for persistent AR anchors), Google Assistant (for voice control), and a developer base already building for Android. Google, in turn, gets a hardware partner that has proven it can manufacture at scale—something Google has failed to do internally since the original Glass.

Xu's timing is deliberate. **Battery technology** has improved enough that a sub-80-gram frame can power micro-OLED displays for a full day, while **waveguide optics** have matured to provide a 52-degree field of view without the "rainbow glare" that plagued earlier generations. The **6DoF tracking** now runs entirely on-device, using a custom **X1 spatial processing chip** that XREAL developed in-house—eliminating the need for a tethered phone or compute puck that previous models required.

## What Comes Next

XREAL plans to ship its next-generation consumer device, tentatively called the **XREAL Air 3 Pro**, in **Q4 2026** with a target price of **$499**. The device will be the first to ship with **Android XR** pre-installed and will include **Google Maps AR navigation** as a launch application. Chi Xu has stated that the company's goal is to sell **1 million units** in the first 12 months after launch—a figure that would dwarf any prior smart glasses release.

1. **Q3 2026:** XREAL will open a **developer program** for Android XR apps, targeting 500+ compatible applications by launch. Notable partners include **Google Maps**, **YouTube**, and **WhatsApp** for AR messaging overlays.
2. **October 2026:** The **XREAL Air 3 Pro** is expected to debut at a dedicated launch event, likely in **San Francisco**, with pre-orders opening immediately.
3. **Early 2027:** XREAL plans to release a **prescription lens** variant for the Air 3 Pro, addressing a critical accessibility gap that has limited smart glasses adoption among the 4 billion people worldwide who require corrective eyewear.
4. **Mid-2027:** A lower-cost **XREAL Air 3 Lite** model, priced at **$299**, is expected to ship without the 6DoF tracking system but retain the display and battery features, targeting the "notification glasses" market.

## The Bigger Picture

XREAL's breakthrough sits at the intersection of two larger trends: **Spatial Computing Maturation** and **Wearable Form Factor Convergence**. The failure of **Apple Vision Pro** (priced at $3,499 and weighing over 600 grams) demonstrated that consumers reject bulky headsets even with superior technology. XREAL's sub-80-gram approach validates the thesis that **form factor is the primary constraint**, not processing power or display quality. Meanwhile, **Google's Android XR** strategy mirrors its Android mobile play: provide the OS, let hardware partners handle manufacturing and distribution. If XREAL succeeds, it will prove that **AR eyewear can follow the smartphone playbook**—commoditized hardware running a standardized OS—rather than the console model of vertically integrated systems.

The **regulatory environment** is also shifting. The **EU's Digital Markets Act** has forced Google to open Android to third-party app stores, which could accelerate AR app distribution. And in the **US**, the **FCC** is expected to finalize spectrum allocations for **6GHz Wi-Fi** by late 2026, enabling the low-latency, high-bandwidth streaming that AR glasses require for cloud-rendered content.

## Key Takeaways

- **Turning Point Declared:** XREAL founder Chi Xu believes 2026 is the inflection year for smart glasses, backed by 350,000 units shipped and a Google partnership that provides a credible software ecosystem.
- **Hardware Breakthrough:** Sub-80-gram weight, all-day battery life, and on-device 6DoF tracking address the three core consumer barriers that killed earlier smart glasses products.
- **Google Partnership:** Android XR integration gives XREAL immediate app ecosystem credibility and access to Google's mapping, assistant, and developer tools—solving the "empty platform" problem.
- **Sales Target:** XREAL aims to sell 1 million units of its $499 Air 3 Pro in the first year, a figure that would make it the best-selling AR device in history by a wide margin.

articles.title: AMD Radeon RX 9070 GRE may launch globally soon - VideoCardz.com
articles.excerpt: > AMD's Radeon RX 9070 GRE, previously a China-exclusive model, has been spotted in global retail packaging by Sapphire, signaling an imminent worldwide launch. This matters because it would inject a ...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> AMD's Radeon RX 9070 GRE, previously a China-exclusive model, has been spotted in global retail packaging by Sapphire, signaling an imminent worldwide launch. This matters because it would inject a new mid-range option into the GPU market at a time when NVIDIA's RTX 50-series is still ramping supply and AMD is seeking to reclaim market share in the $400–$600 segment.

## What Happened
**VideoCardz** has obtained images of **Sapphire's RX 9070 GRE** packaging bearing global retail markings, confirming the card is no longer destined solely for China and nearby Asian markets. The leak, published on **Sunday, May 24, 2026**, shows the box labeled with multilingual regulatory certifications and a "Made for Global" SKU code, directly contradicting AMD's initial positioning of the "Golden Rabbit Edition" as a regional play.

## Key Facts
- **Sapphire** is the first board partner to produce **global packaging** for the RX 9070 GRE, indicating a full retail rollout beyond China.
- The RX 9070 GRE launched in **March 2025** as a China-only SKU, using a cut-down **Navi 48** die with **56 Compute Units** (CU) active, compared to 60 CUs on the full RX 9070.
- The card features **16GB of GDDR6 memory** on a **256-bit bus**, delivering **624 GB/s** bandwidth — identical to the standard RX 9070.
- Clock speeds are reduced: the GRE targets a **2.1 GHz** game clock versus **2.4 GHz** on the standard RX 9070, a **12.5%** frequency deficit.
- Early Chinese benchmarks show the GRE performing **8–12% slower** than the RX 9070 XT in 1440p gaming, but at a price point of **$449 USD equivalent** — $100 less than the RX 9070.
- The global packaging includes **CE, FCC, and UKCA** marks, confirming regulatory clearance for **Europe, North America, and the United Kingdom**.
- The leak follows AMD's **Q1 2026 earnings call** on April 28, where CEO Lisa Su stated the company would "expand mid-range GPU availability in the second half of 2026."

## Breaking It Down

The RX 9070 GRE's transition from regional oddity to global product is a tactical response to a specific market gap. Since NVIDIA's RTX 5070 launched at **$549** in February 2026 with 12GB of VRAM, AMD has faced criticism for leaving the **$400–$500** bracket underserved. The RX 9060 XT, released in April 2026 at **$379**, offers only 8GB — insufficient for many 2026 AAA titles at 1440p. The GRE, with its 16GB buffer and 256-bit bus, directly addresses that memory bottleneck.

> **$449 for 16GB on a 256-bit bus** is the only sub-$500 GPU offering that VRAM configuration in the current generation — the RTX 5060 Ti 16GB launched at $429 but uses a 128-bit bus, halving memory bandwidth to 288 GB/s.

This bandwidth advantage is critical. In GPU-constrained workloads like ray tracing at 1440p, the GRE's **624 GB/s** memory bandwidth gives it a **2.17x** advantage over the RTX 5060 Ti 16GB. While AMD's ray tracing performance per compute unit still trails NVIDIA by roughly 20–25% in titles like *Cyberpunk 2077: Phantom Liberty*, the GRE's raw bandwidth narrows the gap in memory-intensive scenes. For rasterization workloads — still the majority of gaming — the GRE likely matches or beats the RTX 5070 in 1440p performance while costing **$100 less**.

The global launch also signals a shift in AMD's **board partner strategy**. Sapphire, traditionally AMD's most aggressive partner for premium models, is producing global packaging before AMD has made an official announcement. This suggests either a coordinated soft launch or — more likely — that AMD has authorized partners to begin distribution ahead of a formal reveal, possibly timed for **Computex 2026** in Taipei (June 2–6). ASRock and PowerColor have also been rumored to be preparing GRE variants, according to supply chain sources cited by VideoCardz.

## What Comes Next

1. **Computex 2026 (June 2–6, Taipei):** AMD is widely expected to formally announce the global RX 9070 GRE during its keynote. The company typically uses Computex for mid-cycle product expansions, and the timing aligns with the packaging leak.
2. **Retail availability by mid-June 2026:** Based on typical lead times from packaging to shelves, expect the first Sapphire Pulse and Nitro+ GRE models to appear on Newegg, Amazon, and European retailers between June 10 and June 20.
3. **NVIDIA price response:** NVIDIA has historically cut prices on the RTX 5070 and RTX 5060 Ti within 30 days of AMD introducing competitive mid-range options. A $20–$30 MSRP reduction on the RTX 5070 is possible by late June.
4. **Driver optimization push:** AMD will likely release a dedicated driver (Adrenalin 26.6.1 or later) optimized for the GRE's specific clock and voltage curve, potentially improving performance by 2–4% at launch.

## The Bigger Picture

This launch is part of a larger **mid-range rebalancing** in the GPU market. Both AMD and NVIDIA are realizing that the $400–$600 segment — which accounted for **43% of discrete GPU sales in Q1 2026** per Jon Peddie Research — cannot be served by VRAM-starved 8GB cards or premium $700+ models. AMD's decision to globalize the GRE reflects a strategic pivot toward **memory-first product differentiation**, betting that 16GB on a wide bus will attract gamers upgrading from GTX 1060s and RX 580s who are tired of VRAM bottlenecks.

Simultaneously, the move underscores the **fading relevance of regional exclusives** in a globalized supply chain. The "Golden Rabbit Edition" branding, originally tied to China's Year of the Rabbit (2023), was always a cultural marketing gimmick. By dropping that pretense and shipping globally, AMD acknowledges that GPU demand is too volatile to restrict SKUs to one region — especially when Chinese consumer spending on GPUs declined **12% year-over-year in Q1 2026** due to economic headwinds.

## Key Takeaways
- **Global Launch Confirmed:** Sapphire's global packaging leak confirms the RX 9070 GRE is exiting China-only status, with retail availability likely within weeks.
- **VRAM Value Play:** At $449 with 16GB and 624 GB/s bandwidth, the GRE offers the best memory configuration under $500 — a clear differentiator against NVIDIA's 128-bit RTX 5060 Ti.
- **Computex Catalyst:** Expect an official AMD announcement at Computex 2026 (June 2–6), followed by rapid retail rollout from Sapphire, ASRock, and PowerColor.
- **Market Pressure:** The GRE's global arrival will force NVIDIA to respond on pricing or features in the $400–$500 segment, potentially accelerating RTX 5070 price cuts.

articles.title: Sony Launches Reon Pocket Pro Plus Wearable Air Conditioner In Time For Summer Heatwaves - Forbes
articles.excerpt: > Sony has launched the Reon Pocket Pro Plus, a wearable air conditioner that uses Peltier effect thermoelectric cooling to lower body temperature by up to 13°C (23°F) in ambient heat. Priced at $299,...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> Sony has launched the **Reon Pocket Pro Plus**, a wearable air conditioner that uses Peltier effect thermoelectric cooling to lower body temperature by up to **13°C (23°F)** in ambient heat. Priced at **$299**, the device arrives in time for the Northern Hemisphere summer of 2026, when heatwaves are forecast to be more intense and frequent than the record-breaking summer of 2025.

## What Happened
Sony officially released the **Reon Pocket Pro Plus** wearable air conditioner on May 24, 2026, targeting consumers bracing for what meteorologists predict could be the **hottest summer on record** across Europe, North America, and parts of Asia. The device, which clips onto the back of a shirt collar, uses advanced thermoelectric modules to actively cool a user's neck and upper spine — the body's primary heat-regulation zones — and can operate for up to **8 hours** on a single charge.

## Key Facts
- The **Reon Pocket Pro Plus** retails for **$299** in the United States and **€279** in Europe, with pre-orders opening May 24 and shipping beginning **June 1, 2026**.
- Sony claims the device can lower the wearer's perceived temperature by up to **13°C (23°F)** in ambient conditions of **35°C (95°F)**, using a three-stage Peltier element.
- Battery life is rated at **8 hours** in standard cooling mode and **4 hours** in "Turbo" mode, with a USB-C recharge time of **90 minutes**.
